questions.stem: Medicare eligibility is based mainly on age (65 and older), while Medicaid eligibility is based mainly on income.
questions.feedback: Medicare is primarily for people 65+, whereas Medicaid eligibility is based on income and household size.

questions.stem: Medicaid is primarily intended for people with low income.
questions.feedback: Medicaid provides health coverage to eligible low-income adults, children, pregnant women, elderly adults, and people with disabilities.

questions.stem: Medicare's basic rules are set separately by each individual state.
questions.feedback: Medicare is a purely federal program administered nationwide by CMS, so its basic rules are the same in every state.

questions.stem: Medicaid is funded jointly by the federal government and the states.
questions.feedback: Medicaid is a joint federal-state program; the federal share is set by the Federal Medical Assistance Percentage (FMAP).

questions.stem: Medicare is administered nationwide by CMS, a federal agency within HHS.
questions.feedback: Medicare is administered by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), a federal agency within HHS.

questions.stem: Original Medicare is the main program that pays for long-term nursing home (custodial) care.
questions.feedback: Medicaid, not Medicare, is the primary payer for long-term services and supports; Original Medicare generally does not cover long-term custodial care.

questions.stem: Medicaid is the primary payer for long-term services and supports in the United States.
questions.feedback: Medicaid is the nation's primary payer for long-term services and supports (LTSS), including nursing home care.

questions.stem: Parts A, B, C, and D are all parts of Medicare.
questions.feedback: Medicare has four parts: A (hospital), B (medical), C (Medicare Advantage), and D (prescription drugs).

questions.stem: Medicaid is divided into Part A and Part B, just like Medicare.
questions.feedback: Medicaid does not use the lettered Part A/B/C/D structure; that structure belongs to Medicare. Medicaid's companion program for children is CHIP.

questions.stem: Medicare Part C (Medicare Advantage) plans are offered by private companies approved by Medicare.
questions.feedback: Medicare Advantage (Part C) is an alternative to Original Medicare offered by Medicare-approved private companies.

questions.stem: CHIP provides low-cost health coverage to children.
questions.feedback: The Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP) provides low-cost coverage to children in families that earn too much for Medicaid but cannot afford private insurance.

questions.stem: A person can be enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid at the same time.
questions.feedback: People enrolled in both programs are called 'dually eligible' individuals, so a person can have both at once.

questions.stem: For someone enrolled in both programs, Medicaid pays first and Medicare pays last.
questions.feedback: For dual eligibles, Medicare pays first for services it covers and Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort.

questions.stem: Medicare Part B requires a monthly premium.
questions.feedback: Medicare Part B (Medical Insurance) requires a monthly premium; for 2026 the standard premium is $202.90.

questions.stem: Because Medicaid is run by the states, eligibility and covered benefits can vary from state to state.
questions.feedback: Each state administers its own Medicaid program within federal rules, so eligibility details and benefits can vary by state.

questions.stem: Which program is primarily for people age 65 and older?
questions.feedback: Medicare is the federal program primarily for people age 65 and older.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicare is the federal program primarily for people age 65 and older.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicaid is based on income, not age.

options.label: C
options.feedback: CHIP covers children, not seniors.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The Marketplace sells private plans; it is not an age-based program.

questions.stem: Which program bases eligibility mainly on a person's income?
questions.feedback: Medicaid eligibility is based mainly on income and household size.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicare is based mainly on age, not income.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicaid eligibility is based mainly on income and household size.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Part C is a way to receive Medicare benefits, still age-based.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Part D is Medicare drug coverage, still age-based.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes how the two programs are administered?
questions.feedback: Medicare is administered federally by CMS (uniform nationwide), while Medicaid is administered by each state within federal rules (so it varies).

options.label: A
options.text: Both are purely federal and uniform nationwide
options.feedback: Only Medicare is uniform; Medicaid varies by state.

options.label: B
options.text: Both are run entirely by the states
options.feedback: Medicare is federal, not state-run.

options.label: C
options.text: Medicare is federal and uniform; Medicaid is state-run and varies by state
options.feedback: Medicare is administered federally by CMS (uniform nationwide), while Medicaid is administered by each state within federal rules (so it varies).

options.label: D
options.text: Medicare is state-run; Medicaid is purely federal
options.feedback: This reverses the two programs.

questions.stem: How is Medicaid funded?
questions.feedback: Medicaid is funded jointly by the federal government and the states, with the federal share set by the FMAP.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid funding is shared, not purely federal; Medicare is the purely federal program.

options.label: B
options.feedback: States share the cost with the federal government, not alone.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Employer premiums fund commercial insurance, not Medicaid.

options.label: D
options.text: Jointly by the federal government and the states
options.feedback: Medicaid is funded jointly by the federal government and the states, with the federal share set by the FMAP.

questions.stem: Which agency administers Medicare across the country?
questions.feedback: Medicare is administered nationwide by CMS, a federal agency within HHS.

options.label: A
options.text: The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS)
options.feedback: Medicare is administered nationwide by CMS, a federal agency within HHS.

options.label: B
options.feedback: States administer Medicaid, not Medicare.

options.label: C
options.feedback: The IRS handles taxes, not Medicare administration.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Private companies offer some plans but do not administer the federal program.

questions.stem: Which program is the main payer for long-term nursing home care in the U.S.?
questions.feedback: Medicaid is the nation's primary payer for long-term services and supports, including nursing home care.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Original Medicare generally does not cover long-term custodial care.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicaid is the nation's primary payer for long-term services and supports, including nursing home care.

options.label: C
options.feedback: CHIP covers children, not long-term care.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Commercial plans rarely cover extended long-term care.

questions.stem: A patient needs several years of custodial nursing-home care. Which program is most likely to pay for it?
questions.feedback: Long-term custodial care is generally paid by Medicaid, the primary payer for long-term services and supports.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Part A covers short inpatient hospital stays, not long-term custodial care.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Part B covers outpatient and doctor services, not long-term care.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Long-term custodial care is generally paid by Medicaid, the primary payer for long-term services and supports.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Part D covers prescription drugs, not long-term care.

questions.stem: Which list correctly names the four parts of Medicare?
questions.feedback: Medicare has four parts: A, B, C, and D.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicare parts are lettered, not numbered.

options.label: B
options.text: Part A and Part B only
options.feedback: A and B are only Original Medicare; C and D also exist.

options.label: C
options.text: Part A, Part B, Part C, Part D, and Part E
options.feedback: There is no Part E.

options.label: D
options.text: Part A, Part B, Part C, Part D
options.feedback: Medicare has four parts: A, B, C, and D.

questions.stem: Medicare Part C is also known by what name?
questions.feedback: Medicare Part C is also called Medicare Advantage, offered by Medicare-approved private companies.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicare Part C is also called Medicare Advantage, offered by Medicare-approved private companies.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medigap is separate supplemental insurance, not Part C.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Medicaid is a different program entirely.

options.label: D
options.feedback: CHIP is the children's program on the Medicaid side.

questions.stem: What does Medicare Part D cover?
questions.feedback: Medicare Part D is optional prescription drug coverage offered through private companies approved by Medicare.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Inpatient hospital care is Part A.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicare Part D is optional prescription drug coverage offered through private companies approved by Medicare.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Long-term care is mainly covered by Medicaid.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Doctor visits are Part B.

questions.stem: Which statement about Medicaid's structure is correct?
questions.feedback: Medicaid does not use lettered parts; its companion program for children is CHIP.

options.label: A
options.text: Medicaid is divided into Parts A, B, C, and D like Medicare
options.feedback: Lettered parts belong to Medicare, not Medicaid.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicaid covers a broad range of services, not only drugs.

options.label: C
options.text: Medicaid has no lettered parts and pairs with CHIP for children
options.feedback: Medicaid does not use lettered parts; its companion program for children is CHIP.

options.label: D
options.feedback: The two programs differ in eligibility, funding, and structure.

questions.stem: Whom does CHIP primarily cover?
questions.feedback: CHIP provides low-cost coverage to children in families earning too much for Medicaid but unable to afford private insurance.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Seniors are served by Medicare, not CHIP.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Veterans have separate VA coverage.

options.label: C
options.feedback: CHIP covers children, not employers.

options.label: D
options.text: Children in families that earn too much for Medicaid but cannot afford private insurance
options.feedback: CHIP provides low-cost coverage to children in families earning too much for Medicaid but unable to afford private insurance.

questions.stem: Which statement about cost is true?
questions.feedback: Medicare Part B requires a monthly premium ($202.90 in 2026), plus deductibles and coinsurance for many services.

options.label: A
options.text: Medicare Part B has a monthly premium
options.feedback: Medicare Part B requires a monthly premium ($202.90 in 2026), plus deductibles and coinsurance for many services.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicare usually involves some cost, such as the Part B premium.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Part B does charge a premium.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicare, not Medicaid, is the program with regular premiums.

questions.stem: For a person with very low income, which program typically costs the patient little or nothing?
questions.feedback: Medicaid is designed for people with limited income and generally costs the beneficiary little or nothing.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicare usually involves premiums and cost-sharing.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicaid is designed for people with limited income and generally costs the beneficiary little or nothing.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Commercial plans charge premiums.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Part C is a Medicare option and can involve costs.

questions.stem: People who are enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid at the same time are called what?
questions.feedback: People enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid are called dually eligible (or dual eligible) individuals.

options.label: A
options.feedback: A dependent is a person covered under another's plan.

options.label: B
options.feedback: A subscriber is the primary policyholder of one plan.

options.label: C
options.feedback: People enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid are called dually eligible (or dual eligible) individuals.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Self-pay patients have no insurance coverage.

questions.stem: For a dual-eligible person, which program pays first for a covered service?
questions.feedback: For dual eligibles, Medicare pays first for services it covers.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort, paying after Medicare.

options.label: B
options.feedback: CHIP covers children, not dual-eligible adults.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Coverage pays before the patient is billed for covered services.

options.label: D
options.feedback: For dual eligibles, Medicare pays first for services it covers.

questions.stem: The phrase 'payer of last resort' for dual eligibles refers to which program?
questions.feedback: Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort, paying after Medicare and other coverage.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort, paying after Medicare and other coverage.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicare pays first, not last.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Commercial insurance is not the dual-eligible context here.

options.label: D
options.feedback: CHIP is the children's program, not the last-resort payer.

questions.stem: For dual eligibles, Medicaid often helps pay which Medicare costs?
questions.feedback: For dually eligible individuals, Medicaid helps pay Medicare premiums and cost-sharing and covers gaps like long-term care.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid does help with Medicare costs for dual eligibles.

options.label: B
options.feedback: For dually eligible individuals, Medicaid helps pay Medicare premiums and cost-sharing and covers gaps like long-term care.

options.label: C
options.text: Only prescriptions bought outside the U.S.
options.feedback: Medicaid's help is not limited to drugs purchased abroad.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicaid's assistance is far broader than meal charges.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts the two programs?
questions.feedback: Medicare is based mainly on age (65+), while Medicaid is based mainly on income.

options.label: A
options.text: Medicare is income-based; Medicaid is age-based
options.feedback: This reverses the two programs.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Only Medicare is age-based.

options.label: C
options.text: Medicare is age-based; Medicaid is income-based
options.feedback: Medicare is based mainly on age (65+), while Medicaid is based mainly on income.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Only Medicaid is income-based.

questions.stem: A financially well-off 70-year-old retiree is generally eligible for which program?
questions.feedback: Medicare eligibility is based on age, so a 70-year-old qualifies regardless of income.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid is income-based; a well-off person generally would not qualify.

options.label: B
options.feedback: CHIP is for children.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Age 65+ qualifies a person for Medicare.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicare eligibility is based on age, so a 70-year-old qualifies regardless of income.

questions.stem: A low-income 30-year-old parent with little income might qualify for which program?
questions.feedback: Medicaid is income-based, so a low-income adult may qualify even though they are far from age 65.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid is income-based, so a low-income adult may qualify even though they are far from age 65.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Medicare is mainly for people 65+ or with certain disabilities.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Part B is a component of Medicare, still age-based.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicare Advantage is a Medicare option, still age-based.

questions.stem: A Medicare Advantage (Part C) plan typically bundles which coverage together?
questions.feedback: Medicare Advantage plans are bundled and include Part A and Part B and usually Part D drug coverage.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Advantage plans include more than just Part A.

options.label: B
options.text: Part A, Part B, and usually Part D
options.feedback: Medicare Advantage plans are bundled and include Part A and Part B and usually Part D drug coverage.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Drug coverage is usually included but is not the whole plan.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Long-term care is generally a Medicaid responsibility.

questions.stem: Why can Medicaid benefits differ depending on the state you live in?
questions.feedback: Each state administers its own Medicaid program within federal requirements, so eligibility and benefits can vary.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid is not federally uniform; that describes Medicare.

options.label: B
options.feedback: States, not individual hospitals, run Medicaid.

options.label: C
options.text: Because each state runs its own Medicaid program within federal rules
options.feedback: Each state administers its own Medicaid program within federal requirements, so eligibility and benefits can vary.

options.label: D
options.text: Because benefits are priced by ZIP code
options.feedback: Variation comes from state administration, not ZIP-code pricing.

questions.stem: Which program has essentially the same basic rules in every state?
questions.feedback: Medicare is federally administered by CMS, so its basic rules are uniform nationwide.

options.label: A
options.feedback: Medicaid varies by state.

options.label: B
options.feedback: CHIP is state-administered and varies.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Employer plans differ by employer.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Medicare is federally administered by CMS, so its basic rules are uniform nationwide.

questions.stem: Which is a key difference in what the two programs cover?
questions.feedback: Both cover doctors and hospitals, but Medicaid is the primary payer for long-term care, which Original Medicare generally does not cover.

options.label: A
options.text: Medicaid, not Medicare, is the main payer for long-term care
options.feedback: Both cover doctors and hospitals, but Medicaid is the primary payer for long-term care, which Original Medicare generally does not cover.

options.label: B
options.feedback: Both programs cover doctor visits.

options.label: C
options.feedback: Both programs cover hospital care.

options.label: D
options.feedback: Both cover preventive services.
